steps.prompt: Which word is the finite verb?

hints.fidem: 'Fidem' is a noun meaning 'faith' in the accusative
hints.granum: 'Granum' is a noun meaning 'grain' in the accusative
hints.sinapis: 'Sinapis' is a noun meaning 'mustard' in the genitive

steps.reveal: habueritis = you will have had (in conditional protasis)

hints.perfect subjunctive: The forms are identical — in a future conditional with 'si' this functions as future perfect
hints.pluperfect subjunctive: Pluperfect subjunctive would be 'habuissetis' — 'habueritis' lacks the -isse- marker
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'habeatis' — 'habueritis' has the perfect stem

hints.habito, habitare, habitavi, habitatum: These are the parts of 'habitare' (to dwell)
hints.abeo, abire, abii, abitum: These are the parts of 'abire' (to go away)
hints.debeo, debere, debui, debitum: These are the parts of 'debere' (to owe)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'fides' — the -em ending marks accusative
hints.dative: Dative would be 'fidei' — 'fidem' is accusative, direct object

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'fidei' ends in -ei
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'fidei' ends in -ei
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'fidei' ends in -ei

steps.reveal: granum = grain (acc., object of comparison with 'sicut')

hints.nominative: 'Granum' could be nominative, but 'sicut granum' is a comparative phrase — accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'grano' — 'granum' is accusative in the comparison
hints.vocative: One does not address a grain — 'granum' is accusative

hints.relative pronoun: Relative pronouns are 'qui, quae, quod' — 'sicut' is a comparative conjunction
hints.noun meaning 'manner': 'Sicut' is not a noun — it is an adverb/conjunction of comparison

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'sinapi' or 'sinapis' — here it modifies 'granum' as genitive
hints.dative: Dative does not fit — 'sinapis' tells what kind of grain: 'grain of mustard'
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'sinapi' — 'sinapis' is genitive here

translation: If you have faith like a grain of mustard
